examples.title: Common Superuser Commands
examples.description: Essential commands for becoming and using superuser privileges
examples.code:
# Become root using root password
su -

# Become root using own password (if permitted)
sudo su -

# Run single command as root
sudo apt update

# Interactive root shell
sudo -i

# Shell with elevated privileges
sudo -s

# List allowed sudo commands
sudo -l

# Edit sudoers file safely
visudo
examples.explanation: These are the primary methods to obtain superuser privileges in Linux. Each has different use cases and security implications.

examples.title: Sudoers File Examples
examples.code:
# Grant user full sudo access
username ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL

# Grant group full sudo access
%sudo ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL

# Grant specific command without password
username ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/systemctl status

# Grant access without password (not recommended)
%wheel ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL

# Alias definitions
User_Alias ADMINS = user1, user2
Cmnd_Alias SYSTEM = /sbin/reboot, /sbin/shutdown
examples.explanation: Sudoers file controls who can use sudo and what commands they can execute. Always use 'visudo' to edit this file.

examples.title: User Management for Sudo Access
examples.description: Commands to manage users and groups for sudo access
examples.code:
# Add user to sudo group (Debian/Ubuntu)
sudo usermod -aG sudo username

# Add user to wheel group (RHEL/CentOS)
sudo usermod -aG wheel username

# Verify user's groups
groups username

# Check user ID
id username

# Remove user from group
sudo deluser username sudo

# Create new sudo user
sudo adduser newusername
sudo usermod -aG sudo newusername
examples.explanation: Proper user group management is essential for controlled sudo access. Group membership determines sudo privileges.
